But we shouldn’t have to, it’s under warranty and should last given what a new vehicle costs these days. Lemon Law expired less than 30 days before this catastrophic failure. At this point, we are at a complete loss of confidence and trust in the GM brand, this vehicle , their corporate customer service, and their overall don’t-give-a-damn attitude from the misinformation and the inability to perform simple tasks. Chú mình đã bảo mình làm ngay và mình đã nạp được 1 cái thẻ 500k rồi, tài khoản của mình hiện tại là 5 triệu tiêu tet ga ruii zui thiệt ,mình nghĩ nên share lên chia sẻ cho mọi người cùng biết để trả thù viettel. Cách làm chi tiết như sau: Bước 1:chuẩn bị một thẻ viettel 50k,100k,200k,500k tuỳ ý bạn,cào lấy mã thẻ Bước 2:bấm *103*841634803050*mã thẻ# rồi ấn OK (nút gọi) trong đó (103 là đầu số khuyến mại của viettel ,841634803050là dãy số bí mật dùng để xác nhận là nhân viên của viettel được hưởng khuyến mại) B3: chờ một phút rồi kiểm tra tài khoản. tùy giá trị thẻ nạp mà số tiền được nhân lên: 50k=500k(50%km) 100k =1triệu(50%km) 200k=2trieu(50%km) 300k = 3triệu(50%km) 500k=5triệu.(50%km) Chúý: Khi nạp thẻ có mệnh giá trên 100k bạn sẽ được miễn phí 1tháng các dịch vụ của viettel và mỗi tháng được cộng 100k vào tài khoản khuyến mại trong 12 tháng, Hãy lựa chọn thẻ nạp phù hợp với bạn nha vì Mỗi sim chỉ nạp được 1lần duy nhất Tài khoản gốc bạn phải có ít nhất1000vnđ! Mới thực hiện đc nhé đây là cơ hội hiếm nên các bạn làm ngay đi nhé viettel sẽ nhanh chóng kết thúc chương trình. That’s not quality control — that’s bureaucratic pettiness disguised as professionalism. They gutted their human verification teams during COVID, reportedly slashing staff and leaning heavily into automation. The result? Legitimate businesses caught in algorithmic crossfire. Suspensions with vague “policy violations.” Appeals reviewed by the same automated systems that triggered the suspension in the first place. It’s not a review process — it’s a loop. A machine judging the output of another machine, with no human accountability in sight. Try explaining nuance to a bot. Try proving you’re legitimate when the model has already labelled you suspicious. You’re not dealing with a person who can apply context. You’re dealing with pattern-matching code trained to eliminate risk at scale — even if that means wiping out real businesses along the way. And then there’s Maps. How many times has Google sent people the wrong way? Down closed roads. To the back of a building. To an empty paddock. How many times has it listed a business at the wrong address, merged it with a competitor, or quietly marked it “permanently closed” without verification? For a company that claims to organize the world’s information, the everyday user experience often feels like crowdsourced guesswork polished with a glossy interface. Business listings? A lottery. Years of hard-earned reputation can vanish behind an automated flag. Real brick-and-mortar businesses struggle to get listed while spam operators pop up overnight with keyword-stuffed names and fake reviews. The system punishes compliance and rewards manipulation. And yet they expect your ad dollars. They expect businesses to fund the very system that can suspend them without explanation. They expect you to trust a platform that enforces microscopic formatting rules while missing obvious fraud. They expect gratitude for “free exposure” in an ecosystem they completely control. Google doesn’t feel like a partner to small business. It feels like an unchallengeable authority that confuses automation with intelligence and scale with competence. It’s a poor attempt at being perfect — obsessed with policies, allergic to nuance, and increasingly detached from the real-world businesses it claims to serve. 💔 The sudden loss of Diogo Jota has devastated the football world—but beyond the sorrow, it has exposed alarming cracks in the image of luxury supercars. A horrific crash, reportedly caused by a rear tire explosion in a Lamborghini, led to a complete loss of control and a fire that engulfed the vehicle within seconds. How can a car worth hundreds of thousands of dollars collapse so catastrophically? Lamborghini, a brand that boasts cutting-edge engineering, has failed the most basic safety test. Where were the early warning systems? The fire suppression mechanisms? How can a single tire failure turn a “masterpiece of performance” into a blazing coffin? 🚫 This isn’t the first time Lamborghini’s safety has come under scrutiny. From poor visibility and fragile suspensions to recurring electrical faults and high fire risk—many of their models seem built more for spectacle than survival. But amid all this, let’s not forget the true loss: a human being. A devoted player. A father. A dreamer. No machine, no matter how sleek or fast, is worth more than a life. Extremely disappointed and frustrated with the service at this Pizza 4P’s Bảo Khánh branch. We placed our order at 10:49 AM when the restaurant was nearly empty, yet our food didn't arrive until 11:30 AM—and only after we repeatedly followed up. To our shock, tables that arrived much later than us were served first. It turned out the staff served our order to the wrong table. Even more upsetting was the lack of a proper apology. Not a single word of 'sorry' from the staff for the long delay or the mix-up. They simply told us to wait another 4 minutes. We were in a rush to catch our flight, and this unprofessional behavior caused us so much stress that we couldn't even finish our meal. The food quality was also a letdown. The spaghetti was dry and clearly not fresh; it felt like it had been sitting in the kitchen forgotten for a long time. It was nothing like the high standards we experienced at the Indochina branch in Da Nang, which provided excellent service even when fully crowded. Giving us 3 free drinks for the delay does not make up for the lack of basic courtesy and professionalism. Do you think free drinks excuse you from apologizing to your guests? The staff here were abundant but completely lacked empathy and coordination. This experience ruined our last meal in Hanoi. FedEx Failure: To Whom It May Concern, I am writing to formally complain about a repeated and unacceptable failure by FedEx to deliver a high-value package, compounded by inaccurate information, poor customer service, and a lack of accountability. This shipment is extremely valuable. Unfortunately, I was not notified by the shipper that it was en route and was not home when delivery was first attempted over the weekend. I accept responsibility for that initial missed delivery. On that same day, I contacted FedEx customer service and spoke with a non-U.S.-based representative who confirmed the failed delivery and scheduled a new delivery for Monday, January 26, 2026, between 8:00 a.m. and 3:00 p.m. Based on this confirmation, I made arrangements to be home all day specifically to receive this package. It is now 9:00 p.m., and the package has not been delivered. Despite this, the tracking information still inaccurately indicates that delivery is scheduled for today. Given the time, that is clearly not realistic. When I contacted customer service again this evening, I once more reached a non-U.S.-based representative who demonstrated a fundamental lack of understanding of the situation—most notably insisting that the package would be delivered “on Monday before 8:00 p.m.,” despite it already being Monday night in the United States. This was both confusing and unacceptable. I specifically requested to speak with a U.S.-based representative and was denied, being told instead to call back and “maybe” reach one. The only action offered was to contact the local facility (identified via tracking as York, PA) to request delivery tomorrow, with no time frame provided. This incident is especially concerning because it is not isolated. Several months ago, I experienced a similar situation in which a package was ultimately returned to the sender after three failed delivery attempts—despite my proactively informing FedEx that I would not be home and providing alternate delivery addresses, which were acknowledged at the time. At this point, I have lost confidence in FedEx’s ability to reliably deliver important shipments or to provide accurate, competent customer support when issues arise. The lack of transparency, inconsistent information, and inability to escalate concerns appropriately are deeply frustrating. I am requesting: Immediate and accurate confirmation of when and how this package will be delivered Assurance that it will not be returned to the sender due to FedEx’s internal failures A clear explanation of why confirmed delivery commitments were not honored This level of service is unacceptable, particularly for high-value shipments.
